content.text: Operators are the verbs of JavaScript — they act on values (operands) to produce a new value. JavaScript inherits most of its operators from C/Java but adds several unique ones, and ES2015–2021 introduced modern operators that are now standard in Angular/TypeScript code.
Understanding operator precedence and short-circuit evaluation is especially important in Angular: template expressions, conditional rendering, and reactive operators all rely on these semantics.

attrs.alt: JavaScript operator categories: arithmetic, comparison, logical, assignment, modern operators, precedence, short-circuit

content.text:
// Standard arithmetic
5 + 3    // 8
5 - 3    // 2
5 * 3    // 15
5 / 3    // 1.6666...
5 % 3    // 2  — remainder (modulo)
2 ** 8   // 256 — exponentiation (ES2016)

// Increment and decrement
let x = 5;
x++;     // post-increment: returns 5, then x becomes 6
++x;     // pre-increment: x becomes 7, returns 7
x--;     // post-decrement: returns 7, then x becomes 6
--x;     // pre-decrement: x becomes 5, returns 5

// String concatenation with +
'Hello' + ' ' + 'World'  // 'Hello World'
'Count: ' + 42           // 'Count: 42' — number coerced to string

// Unary +  — converts to number
+'42'       // 42
+true       // 1
+null       // 0
+undefined  // NaN
+''         // 0

// Unary - — negates
-5          // -5
-'3'        // -3 (converts and negates)

content: (remainder) operator is commonly used to cycle through a list or wrap indices:
Comparison operators return a boolean. The most important distinction is between strict equality (

content.text:
// Strict equality — no type coercion
5 === 5          // true
5 === '5'        // false — different types
null === null    // true
null === undefined // false

// Loose equality — coerces types
5 == '5'         // true  — '5' coerced to 5
null == undefined // true  — the only useful case for ==
false == 0       // true  — both falsy
'' == 0          // true  — string to number

// Strict inequality
5 !== '5'        // true
5 !== 5          // false

// Relational
3 < 5            // true
5 >= 5           // true
'abc' < 'abd'    // true — lexicographic comparison
'10' > '9'       // false — string comparison! ('1' < '9')
10 > 9           // true  — numeric comparison

simultaneously. Angular ESLint enforces this via the
Logical operators evaluate boolean conditions, but crucially, they return one of their operands rather than always returning
. This makes them useful as concise expressions.

content.text: && (AND) — Returns First Falsy or Last Value
|| (OR) — Returns First Truthy or Last Value
?? (Nullish Coalescing, ES2020) — Returns Right Side Only for null/undefined

// Evaluates right side ONLY if left is null or undefined
null ?? 'default'      // 'default'
undefined ?? 'default' // 'default'
0 ?? 'default'         // 0  — 0 is not null/undefined
'' ?? 'default'        // ''  — '' is not null/undefined
false ?? 'default'     // false

// ?? is correct for optional numeric config
const timeout = config.timeout ?? 5000;  // 0 is preserved
const port    = options.port    ?? 3000; // explicit 0 would be kept
const label   = props.label     ?? 'Submit'; // '' would be preserved

// Chaining
const value = a ?? b ?? c ?? 'fallback'; // first non-null/undefined

!true         // false
!false        // true
!0            // true  — 0 is falsy
!''           // true  — '' is falsy
!null         // true
!{}           // false — objects are truthy
!!value       // coerce to boolean (double negation)
!!0           // false
!!''          // false
!![]          // true
The conditional (ternary) operator is the only JavaScript operator that takes three operands. It is the primary way to write conditional expressions in Angular templates.

content: operator has two roles depending on context: spread (expands an iterable) and rest (collects remaining items).
. This is one of the most useful modern operators in Angular code.

content.text:
// typeof — returns type string
typeof 'hello'     // 'string'
typeof 42          // 'number'
typeof true        // 'boolean'
typeof undefined   // 'undefined'
typeof null        // 'object'  ← bug; check null === null instead
typeof {}          // 'object'
typeof []          // 'object'  ← use Array.isArray()
typeof function(){} // 'function'

// instanceof — checks prototype chain
[] instanceof Array     // true
{} instanceof Object    // true
new Date() instanceof Date // true

class Animal {}
class Dog extends Animal {}
const d = new Dog();
d instanceof Dog    // true
d instanceof Animal // true (inheritance)

// in — checks if property exists in object (including prototype)
'name' in user       // true if user has 'name' property
'toString' in {}     // true — inherited from Object.prototype
Bitwise operators work on 32-bit integers. Rarely needed in Angular application code, but they appear in performance-critical algorithms, flags, and occasionally in Angular internals.

// Bitwise operators convert operands to 32-bit integers
5 & 3    // 1   — AND:  0101 & 0011 = 0001
5 | 3    // 7   — OR:   0101 | 0011 = 0111
5 ^ 3    // 6   — XOR:  0101 ^ 0011 = 0110
~5       // -6  — NOT:  inverts all bits
5 << 1   // 10  — left shift (multiply by 2)
5 >> 1   // 2   — right shift (divide by 2, keeps sign)
-5 >>> 1 // large positive — unsigned right shift

// Common practical uses
// Fast integer truncation (faster than Math.floor for positive numbers)
~~3.7    // 3   (double NOT)
5 | 0    // 5   (OR with 0 truncates to int)

// Feature flags via bitmask
const READ  = 1;  // 001
const WRITE = 2;  // 010
const EXEC  = 4;  // 100
const perms = READ | WRITE;     // 3 (011)
(perms & READ) !== 0;           // true — has READ
(perms & EXEC) !== 0;           // false — no EXEC

content: When an expression has multiple operators, JavaScript uses precedence rules to determine evaluation order. Use parentheses
to make intent explicit rather than relying on memorised precedence.
Angular's template expression syntax supports most JavaScript operators with a few restrictions: no assignment (

increments first. Avoid in-expression side effects.
is a shallow copy — nested objects are still shared by reference.
The three logical assignment operators combine a logical check with an assignment. They are syntactic sugar but genuinely improve readability for initialisation patterns.
Operator Combinations in Real Angular Code
Operators rarely appear in isolation. Here are patterns you will encounter throughout a real Angular codebase:

Total: {{ (price * quantity * (1 + taxRate)) | currency }}

{{ user?.displayName ?? user?.email ?? 'Anonymous' }} @if (items?.length && !isLoading && !hasError) { }
TypeScript adds a few operators that compile away to JavaScript but provide compile-time safety:

(non-null assertion) only when you have external knowledge that TypeScript cannot verify — for example, after a DOM querySelector you know returns an element because you control the HTML. Avoid it in application logic where a proper null check is feasible.

content: JavaScript provides arithmetic, comparison, logical, assignment, and several modern operators. The most important habits: always use
for null/undefined fallbacks to avoid 0 and empty-string gotchas, use optional chaining
to safely navigate potentially-null object paths, and use spread
to create new array/object references for Angular's change detection. Angular templates support most JavaScript operators except assignment, bitwise, and imperative constructs — the template
is Angular's pipe operator, not bitwise OR.
